annotations.decision: very mild wheezes, decreased breath sounds on the right, no abdominal pain
CXR showed possible PNA and CTA Chest was was negative for PE, but confirmed a small consolidation within the right lower lobe
pt is normally O2 dependent, but was dipping down into the high 80s on his usual 2L NC, along with decreased lung sounds.
Denies chest pain or tightness, palpitations
Denies nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation or abdominal pain
No recent change in bowel or bladder habits
General: Alert, oriented, no acute distress
HEENT: Sclera anicteric, MM dry, oropharynx clear. R eye blue and blind.
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Rhonchi in RLL, but otherwise no wheezes, rales
CV: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, normal S1 + S2, no murmurs, rubs, gallops
Abdomen: softly distended, minimally TTP in LLL, bowel sounds present, no guarding
Ext: Cool, 1+ pulses, no clubbing, cyanosis or edema
Blood Culture, Routine (Preliminary):       STAPHYLOCOCCUS, COAGULASE NEGATIVE

Anaerobic Bottle Gram Stain (Final [**2173-4-12**]):       Reported to and read back by [**First Name4 (NamePattern1) **] [**Last Name (NamePattern1) **] @ 1622 ON [**4-11**] - [**Numeric Identifier 6650**].       GRAM POSITIVE COCCI.       IN CLUSTERS.
CHEST (PORTABLE AP): Low lung volumes with bibasilar atelectasis. Difficult to exclude small right pleural effusion. No definite evidence of pneumonia

CTA CHEST W&W/O C&RECONS, NON-CORONARY: Extensive mediastinal and hilar lymphadenopathy, with narrowing of right lower lobe bronchi, causing moderate right lower lobe atelectasis, with a small right pleural effusion and small right lower lobe consolidation
Multiple right pulmonary nodules measure up to 11 mm. The findings are suspicious for a neoplastic process. Reactive lymphadenopathy from infection is also possible.
No pulmonary embolus detected to the subsegmental levels
Moderate atherosclerotic soft plaque throughout the thoracic aorta
The visualized vessels are patent and compressible
There is arterialization of the SFV waveform with lack of forward flow in diastole which could be in keeping with congestive heart failure
CXR:  FINDINGS: In comparison with study of [**4-13**], there are continued low lung volumes with enlargement of the cardiac silhouette
pulmonary edema.  Bibasilar atelectasis and probable small pleural effusions
there is little change from the previous study.
CXR:  FINDINGS: Mild pulmonary edema and bibasilar atelectasis, left side more than right and small bilateral pleural effusions are unchanged over past 24 hours
Mild pulmonary edema, bibasal atelectasis and small pleural effusions are unchanged over last 24 hours.
CXR:  FINDINGS: Compared to the prior radiograph, there is now mroe prominent interstitial thickening consistent with worsening pulmonary edema
Again seen are small bilateral pleural effusions, cardiomegaly and retrocardiac opacification, likely atelectasis
There is no focal consolidation or pneumothorax
Stable bibasilar atelectasis and pleural effusions
complicated by atrial fibrillation with RVR and hypotension
requiring brief MICU stay for closer monitoring

annotations.category: Category 11: Other - Medical decision to withhold life-sustaining treatment

annotations.decision: Hypoxic Respiratory Distress: Patient presented with hypoxia
Hypoxia occurred in the setting of baseline COPD
patient's poor lung function was most likely exacerbated by a possible right lower lobe pneumonia, noted on CXR but not on CTA
x-ray imaging showed some evidence of pulmonary edema
Patient was evaluated by Speech and Swallow
was determined to have no overt aspiration
CTA showed no evidence of pulmonary embolism
developed afib with RVR and hypotension
treated empirically with vancomycin and Zosyn
he experienced flash pulmonary edema in response to an episode of agitation and hypertension.  His respiratory function continued to be poor
with NRB or high-flow face mask requirement

thought to be due to HCAP, likely post-obstructive, combined with flash pulmonary edema and underlying COPD. He was not able to cough, and therefore was unable to clear any infectious component. His prognosis was thought to be poor given his lack of response to treatment and progressive respiratory decline
He used a highflow facemask for supplemental oxygen
treated with morphine and lorzepam as needed
One set of BCx's did grow CONS, but this was probably a contaminant.
switched to an oral regimen with Augmentin and azithromycin
antibiotics were continued until decision was made to pursue comfort-focused care.
developed RVR with rates up to 200.
with subsequent return of rate to low 100s
Trigger for afib with RVR was likely multifactorial from systemic stress of infection, volume overload and possible hypercarbia
restarted to avoid Afib with RVR and subsequent flashing
started on metoprolol tartrate with good rate control.  This was later held for a day due to hypotensive episode on HD3, then restarted to avoid Afib with RVR and subsequent flashing.

patient developed hypotension to SBPs in the 70s-80s. This was most likely secondary to medication effects and volume overload with relative intravascular repletion. Differential also included pulmonary embolism, myocardial infarction, sepsis and hypovolemia
LLE Doppler was negative for DVT
Patient appeared euvolemic on exam, but exhibited pulmonary edema on CXR.
Pt's plt count now downtrended to 78 at its nadir, but had values in the low 100s (113, 134) in [**6-/2172**] as well
No obvious signs of bleeding. Other possible etiology is possible MDS.
The patient experienced some delirium with reduced orientation.  This was most likely due to pneumonia and respiratory distress.
Increased creatinine from 1.0 to 1.3 on MICU admission, returned to baseline 1.1
Likely result of poor forward flow in the setting of AF with RVR and may have pre-renal given poor po intake,
likely due to history of falls
The patient's respiratory status continued to degrade despite antibiotics and diuresis.  He was uncomfortable on turning and dyspnic with mild exertion.
